← back

2023-12-07

On Faith

A lot of thoughts and ideas have been circling in my head endlessly for the last few days, and only a slow Thursday and an empty office gave me permission to put them down on paper. There’s not much direction here or development for that matter, but that is probably symptomatic of the ideas themselves.



Lately, I’ve been thinking a lot about religion and the distinction between organized and non-organized forms of it. The inherited repulsion to religion owing to my immigrant existence across the fault lines of Bengal, has always pushed me further away from all forms of religious texts and consequently rendered my arguments against religion perfectly anecdotal, weak, and easily refutible. I don’t know what the Bible says about same-sex marriage, I can only show you how it has affected the world. Maybe this perception of mine of how religious texts or the world around it works finds its own counterpart in fanatically religious people; we both have no idea of what is in the book, but we confidently arm ourselves on different sides of it.



My atheist friends would strongly argue, that not every invention and imagery that has been conjured into existence by a power-seeker needs to be studied, verified, or debunked. The responsibility to be truthful lies with the storyteller, it would be quite an endless and tedious pursuit to verify every hallucination of every “patient” in every mental health institution. Although I do not exactly disagree with this point, a distinction must be made for the imagery that has survived (and quite ruthlessly) the test of a few centuries. When you look at the current Hindu nationalist government in India, this matter becomes clearer. The religious tinge is not subtle in their speeches and campaigns, they attack the most unprotected psychological part of the human brain, the fear of the unknown and the other, the existence of which the privileged section of the Indian left even refuses to accept. Two groups of people provoked by this fundamental fear and acting under the perceived threat of non-existence are preached to by the left- don’t be afraid, both of your gods do not exist. Was religion created by the state as a weapon, or was it created by the people, in this primitive need for self-preservation, that we currently do not provide an acceptable alternative for? But I digress…



A lot of my thoughts and arguments often boil down to the one simple question — are humans inherently good or bad? There’s one supplementary question of what it really means to be good or bad, so I may rephrase the question as, do humans necessarily act in self-interest, and only act in the interest of others when a visible chain of events connects it to theirs? I don’t have an answer. Even in my manufactured honesty as an author (of this “essay”), I cannot claim to be a “good” person.



But back to religion. Today morning I had a session with my therapist, and we discussed the current issues I’m facing at my workplace among other daily struggles in my life which I won’t bore you with here. But it made me think, about how I go about my life, and how my perceived rationality is in reality extremely irrational. I’ve noticed quite often, the optimism yet the cold rationality with which the “religious” people I know take on the world, one thing leads to the other, one action secures an event, if it doesn’t, we have to reconsider the next action. God will take care of me. Whereas for me, the world is a landmine of cruel probabilities, nothing guarantees anything, and nothing is assured, every day I play a lottery against the lawlessness of nature and hope for the best. Life is a highly stochastic environment with sparse rewards, and no underlying fixed model you can learn, nothing you can prepare yourself for. One day you’re awaiting a positive response on your job application, and the same morning some unrelated tragic event subsumes your life. Nothing guarantees anything, and you can’t rely on anything. Every event has the potential to ruin your day or life, and you’re armed with nothing as you step onto this chessboard every morning. The “rationality” we atheists take our pride in, is it in reality our extreme helplessness in the irrationality of life? Handing your faith to imagery, to this assurance of certainty, isn’t that a more rational approach to life, in this continuously changing stochastic paradigm of existence?







write your thoughts:
